title: 6423-24-01b - Intake / Fresh CAD review verification CAD Review
commonThemes: Critical mechanisms are mounted outboard of the frame rails rather than recessed inside them.
Multiple functions are concentrated into mirrored side plate/linkage stacks with many pivots and fasteners.
Large spanning members are end-supported with little visible secondary support or guarding.
Side impact bends or breaks the exposed intake fingers or outboard linkage hardware.
Roller or shaft deflection causes uneven compression, inconsistent feeding, or rubbing at one side first.
Tolerance stack-up across mirrored linkage plates causes left/right asymmetry, binding, or angle mismatch.

reviewerTraits.systems_integration: Integration, interfaces, mounting, materials, manufacturability, and dependency risk.
reviewerTraits.program_readiness: Schedule, team coordination, electronics/programming/manufacturing impact, testability, and realistic ship scope.
reviewerTraits.mechanical_robustness: Contact geometry, snagging, flex, impact, field interaction, jam paths, wear, and fatigue risk.
reviewerTraits.synthesis: Merges overlap, preserves disagreement, and turns review findings into prioritized action items.

personaReports.summary: Visible CAD shows a compact intake package, but the main review risks are exposed outboard mechanisms, a wide side-supported roller span, and a linkage-heavy assembly with likely tolerance/service burden. The current captures support interface and manufacturability concerns more strongly than any hard collision or material-failure conclusion.

topConcerns.title: Outboard intake features are exposed beyond the protected chassis envelope
topConcerns.description: In `2026-05-18T00-59-52-921Z_cad-review-evidence-left.png`, `2026-05-18T00-59-54-499Z_cad-review-evidence-right.png`, and the isometric capture, the light-blue intake fingers and adjacent linkage/roller-end hardware sit outside the side rails with no visible guard structure. That creates a direct impact/snag risk at the robot interface boundary and makes these parts the first contact point in side interactions.

topConcerns.title: Upper roller set appears to be supported only at the ends across a large span
topConcerns.description: In `2026-05-18T00-59-51-332Z_cad-review-evidence-isometric.png`, `2026-05-18T00-59-56-083Z_cad-review-evidence-top.png`, and the front baseline, both long white rollers span nearly the full intake width with no visible mid-span support. That geometry increases sensitivity to roller shaft deflection, tube bowing, and alignment drift under load, especially if compression is expected across the full width.

topConcerns.title: Left/right intake actuation is mechanically dense and likely tolerance-sensitive
topConcerns.description: The side views show multi-link plates, multiple pivots, spacers, and several outboard fasteners on both sides of the intake. The mirrored, exposed linkage stacks suggest that left/right synchronization and repeatable assembly will depend on tight plate spacing and pivot alignment; any stack-up error can skew roller position or intake angle.

topConcerns.title: Service access to the intake ends looks constrained by stacked side hardware
topConcerns.description: The roller ends, side plates, and linkage hardware are packaged tightly together in the isometric and side captures, with the roller support hardware nested inside the arm structure. That raises removal and maintenance difficulty for rollers, shafts, and bearings because the likely service path is blocked by other assembled parts.
topConcerns.missingEvidence: Exploded view or assembly order is not visible, so this is based on access geometry only.

repeatedPatterns: Critical mechanisms are mounted outboard of the frame rails rather than recessed inside them.
Multiple functions are concentrated into mirrored side plate/linkage stacks with many pivots and fasteners.
Large spanning members are end-supported with little visible secondary support or guarding.
likelyFailureModes: Side impact bends or breaks the exposed intake fingers or outboard linkage hardware.
Roller or shaft deflection causes uneven compression, inconsistent feeding, or rubbing at one side first.
Tolerance stack-up across mirrored linkage plates causes left/right asymmetry, binding, or angle mismatch.
Maintenance delays occur because routine roller-end service requires partial teardown of adjacent side hardware.
recommendedChanges: Pull the outermost intake fingers/linkage inward or add sacrificial guards/bump structures so they are not first-contact features.
Review roller shaft/span stiffness and add mid-span support or a stiffer shaft/tube strategy if full-width compression is required.
Reduce linkage stack complexity where possible, or add clear hard references/shims so left and right sides assemble repeatably.
Design explicit service paths for roller and bearing removal without disassembling the full side mechanism.
Add protective covers or keep-out structure around exposed roller-end and linkage hardware.

personaReports.summary: Program-readiness risk is moderate. The visible CAD shows a compact, mechanism-dense intake with multiple exposed rollers, linkages, and side plates packed into a small front volume. That supports concerns around integration time, wiring/service access, alignment sensitivity, and realistic first-test scope more than around raw concept viability.

topConcerns.title: Front intake package is dense and access-constrained
topConcerns.description: The isometric and front evidence captures show the two front rollers, side links, and side plates stacked tightly over the intake throat with little obvious hand/tool access from the front or top. That will slow assembly, rework, and mechanism debugging, especially during early programming and tuning.

topConcerns.title: Manufacturing and alignment burden appears high for a first shippable version
topConcerns.description: The left, right, and isometric views show many separate plates, pivots, spacers, fasteners, and roller-end features on both sides. A layout with this many interdependent parts is likely to consume schedule in fabrication, hole-location tolerance stackups, and side-to-side alignment before the mechanism is ready for reliable testing.

topConcerns.title: Visible wire/sensor routing for the moving intake is not defined
topConcerns.description: The top and isometric views show exposed moving side structures and spinning rollers, but no visible cable guides, guarded routing path, or reserved sensor/electronics mounts on the intake itself. That creates cross-team risk because programming and electronics work will depend on late mechanical decisions for wiring and sensing.
topConcerns.missingEvidence: Dedicated electronics layout, sensor models, and wire routing clips/channels are not visible in the provided/current CAD views.

topConcerns.title: Exposed external mechanisms increase early-test reliability risk
topConcerns.description: The side and isometric views show exposed roller ends and side drive/linkage hardware with no visible guarding or containment features. In early driver and programming tests, that raises the chance of snagging game pieces, field elements, or wiring and turning simple tuning sessions into mechanical recovery work.

topConcerns.title: First-test scope looks broader than necessary
topConcerns.description: The CAD already combines a multi-link intake arm, dual upper rollers, and a shaped central funnel structure in one package. Visually, this is more integrated than a minimal first article, which increases coordination load across manufacturing, assembly, software bring-up, and iteration timing.

repeatedPatterns: Mechanism density is concentrated at the front where assembly, tuning, and debugging will also happen.
Both sides rely on multi-part plate/link stacks rather than a visibly simplified single-piece support strategy.
Several critical functions appear exposed rather than packaged for serviceability or protection.
likelyFailureModes: Delayed first powered test because fabrication and alignment of the side assemblies take longer than expected.
Programming blocked by mechanical access problems when roller, linkage, or belt/chain adjustments require partial disassembly.
Wire or sensor routing conflicts with moving intake members during early integration.
Inconsistent intake behavior side-to-side if the mirrored linkage/roller assemblies are not assembled or shimmed identically.
Early practice damage or jams from exposed mechanism edges and rotating elements.
recommendedChanges: Reduce first-release scope by simplifying one degree of mechanism complexity if performance goals allow.
Add explicit wire-routing and sensor-mount features into the CAD before electronics and programming work starts.
Create service openings or tool-clearance access for the roller and linkage adjustment points.
Consolidate side-assembly parts where possible to reduce tolerance stackup and fabrication count.
Define a protected first-test configuration with guards or keep-out treatment around exposed moving elements.

personaReports.summary: Visible CAD evidence supports a moderate robustness risk assessment driven by exposed outboard mechanism, converging intake pinch geometry, and lightly guarded roller-end hardware. The model does not show enough motion-state or section-detail evidence to confirm hard interference, but it does show several contact and snag-prone geometries that are directly exposed to field and game-piece interaction.

topConcerns.title: Outboard snag hook / protruding feature on side mechanism
topConcerns.description: In the right-side view and matching baseline side captures, the light-blue hook-shaped feature extends clearly outside the green side-arm envelope and sits exposed ahead of the main frame line. That creates a direct snag and impact initiator for field elements, cables, or other robots before load can be shared into the chassis.

topConcerns.title: Exposed roller-end linkage and hardware with little guarding
topConcerns.description: The isometric and front captures show the roller-end support/linkage hardware sitting proud of the intake structure, especially near the right roller end. Those small external links/spacers appear to be among the first-contact features in a frontal or corner impact, which raises bending, loosening, and wear risk at the support points.

topConcerns.title: Converging jam/pinch throat below the roller pair
topConcerns.description: The front and top captures show a narrow converging entry between the curved lower panel and the lower roller, with side plates closing in near the roller ends. That visible geometry can trap off-axis or partially folded objects against a hard side boundary instead of self-centering them, increasing jam and local wear risk.
topConcerns.missingEvidence: No motion sweep or representative game-piece overlay is visible, so jam severity cannot be confirmed from static CAD alone.

topConcerns.title: Large unsupported side-arm geometry ahead of the frame
topConcerns.description: The side views show tall single-plate green arms carrying mechanism mass above and ahead of the base rail, with exposed upper hardware near the free end. Even without section data, the geometry suggests impact loads will create a long moment arm back into the pivot/fastener region, making flex and fatigue concentration likely there.
topConcerns.missingEvidence: Plate thickness, material, bearing support stackup, and hard-stop locations are not visible.

repeatedPatterns: Multiple functional parts sit outside the protected chassis envelope instead of behind sacrificial guards.
Several first-contact geometries are small links, roller-end spacers, or hook-like details rather than broad compliant surfaces.
The intake path uses hard converging boundaries near the roller ends, which tends to concentrate contact and wear at the edges.
likelyFailureModes: Snagging on the exposed hook/outboard detail leading to sudden side loads or mechanism pull-back.
Corner or wall impact bending the roller-end support/linkage hardware before the base frame takes load.
Progressive loosening or wear at outboard pivots/spacers from repeated vibration and small impacts on cantilevered hardware.
Off-axis object entry jamming at the side of the roller throat, followed by scuffing or repeated edge wear on the side plates and lower guide.
recommendedChanges: Pull the hook-like outboard feature back inside the arm envelope or add a smooth outer guard/fairing that becomes the first-contact surface.
Add guarding or a sacrificial bumper around roller-end linkage/hardware so impacts resolve into larger structural members.
Open or soften the side-entry throat near the roller ends with chamfers/funnels/compliant guides to reduce edge pinching and jam initiation.
Shorten unsupported load paths where possible, or add triangulation / dual-side support / thicker local reinforcement at the side-arm pivot and upper mechanism mounts.
Define positive hard stops and protected load paths so overtravel or field contact does not react through small exposed links.

mergedActionItems.title: Pull or guard all first-contact outboard intake features
mergedActionItems.description: Revise the intake envelope so hooks, fingers, roller-end links, and similar protrusions are either moved inside the side-arm/chassis boundary or covered by sacrificial guards or smooth bump surfaces. Treat this as the highest-priority geometry change because it is the strongest area of agreement across reviews and directly affects durability and testability.

mergedActionItems.title: Simplify and datum-control the mirrored side linkage assemblies
mergedActionItems.description: Reduce part count where feasible, consolidate plates/spacers, and add explicit assembly datums, shimming strategy, and hard references so left and right sides build repeatably. This follow-up addresses both schedule risk and side-to-side behavior drift without requiring full concept rework.

mergedActionItems.title: Create explicit service paths for roller-end and adjustment access
mergedActionItems.description: Update CAD so rollers, shafts, bearings, and key adjustment points can be reached and removed without major side-mechanism teardown. This should include tool clearance, fastener access, and an intended service order for the front package.

mergedActionItems.title: Run stiffness and load-path verification on rollers and side arms
mergedActionItems.description: Do a targeted verification pass on the full-width upper rollers and the long side-arm supports using shaft deflection checks, support-stack review, and impact/load-path analysis. Preserve the current architecture only if the span and arm geometry are shown to stay within acceptable alignment and durability limits.

mergedActionItems.title: Validate and soften the intake throat against off-axis jams
mergedActionItems.description: Add a motion/game-piece review for the converging lower throat and side-entry regions, then adjust with chamfers, funnels, or compliant guides if off-axis entry can pinch against hard boundaries. This is a concrete follow-up because the jam concern is credible but not fully proven from static views alone.

mergedActionItems.title: Define wiring, sensing, and a reduced first-test configuration now
mergedActionItems.description: Add cable-routing features, protected sensor mount locations, and a clear minimum viable first-test configuration before fabrication releases. If needed, defer nonessential complexity so programming and electronics are not blocked by late mechanical packaging decisions.
